one_table_vs_multiple_tables.label: One Table vs Multiple Tables
one_table_vs_multiple_tables.summary: Learn why some data fits in one table and why larger systems often use several related tables.

sketch0.title: A single table can answer simple questions

sketch1.title: Why databases sometimes use multiple tables

sketch2.title: How to think about one table vs many

sc-one-row-represents.title: What does one row represent?
sc-one-row-represents.prompt: In the `students` table, what does one row represent?
sc-one-row-represents.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove choices from unrelated topics.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

sc-when-multiple-tables.title: When multiple tables help
sc-when-multiple-tables.prompt: When is using multiple tables usually more helpful than one big table?
sc-when-multiple-tables.hint: Think about cases where one big table would repeat lots of information.

help.concept: Multiple tables are useful when data includes different kinds of things or when one large table would duplicate the same details many times.
help.hint_1: Imagine storing students and full class details in every student row.
help.hint_2: Choose the option about reducing repeated information and separating different kinds of data.

options.a: When all columns describe the same single kind of thing
options.b: When you want fewer column names
options.c: When different kinds of information would be repeated in one big table
options.d: When SQL cannot read one table

mc-single-table-signs.title: Signs one table is enough
mc-single-table-signs.prompt: Choose all statements that suggest one table may be enough for a small dataset.
mc-single-table-signs.hint: Look for ideas about one row representing one kind of thing.

help.concept: One table works well when each row represents one clear entity and the columns all describe that same entity directly.
help.hint_1: Pick statements about a focused row meaning and columns that belong together.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices that mention mixing unrelated things into one row.

options.b: The columns all describe that student
options.c: The table mixes students, classes, and teachers in the same row
options.d: You only need details already stored in the student records

mc-reasons-split-tables.title: Reasons to split data into multiple tables
mc-reasons-split-tables.prompt: Choose all good reasons a database might use multiple tables.
mc-reasons-split-tables.hint: Think about organization and repeated information.

help.concept: Databases use multiple tables to separate different kinds of entities and reduce repeated blocks of information.
help.hint_1: Look for options about students versus other kinds of things, and about avoiding duplication.
help.hint_2: Do not choose answers that claim multiple tables are required just because SQL exists.

options.a: Students and classes are different kinds of things
options.b: A big table would repeat the same class details many times
options.c: SQL can only run if there are at least two tables
options.d: Separate tables can make the structure clearer

dr-read-simple-query.title: Read a basic query in order
dr-read-simple-query.prompt: Put these parts in the order you read the query `SELECT name FROM students;` from top to bottom.
dr-read-simple-query.hint: Arrange the pieces by following the order introduced in the lesson.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

dr-think-about-structure.title: Questions to ask about table structure
dr-think-about-structure.prompt: Arrange these questions in a useful order for deciding whether one table makes sense.
dr-think-about-structure.hint: Arrange the pieces by following the order introduced in the lesson.

help.concept: A good table-design thought process starts with the row meaning, then checks whether columns match that meaning, then looks for repeated information that may belong elsewhere.
help.hint_1: First define the row, then inspect the columns, then think about repetition.
help.hint_2: The question about repeated details should come after you know what the row and columns are supposed to represent.

tokens.t2: Do all columns describe that same thing?
tokens.t3: Am I repeating lots of details that might belong elsewhere?

fb-table-name.title: Complete the table source
fb-table-name.prompt: Fill in the missing table name to read student names from the dataset.
fb-table-name.hint: Use the only table shown in the schema browser for this topic.

help.concept: The `FROM` clause tells SQL which table to read. In this lesson, the dataset table is named `the missing term`.
help.hint_1: The table contains columns like `id`, `name`, `grade`, `age`, and `city`.
help.hint_2: Choose the table name that matches the student records in the schema.

fb-column-choice.title: Choose the right column
fb-column-choice.prompt: Fill in the missing column to show where each student lives.
fb-column-choice.hint: Read the words around the blank and choose the term that fits the role.

help.concept: In a `SELECT` query, the chosen column should match the kind of information you want to display. The place each student lives is stored in the the missing term column.
help.hint_1: Read the words around the blank and choose the term that fits the role.
help.hint_2: It is not `grade` or `age`; it stores location names.

help.concept: This guided SQL check uses a read-only SELECT query to help you connect the lesson idea to the results table.
help.hint_1: Start with SELECT, choose the requested column or columns, then name the students table with FROM.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table with the prompt.

ci-show-names-only.hint: Follow the SELECT ... FROM ... pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This guided SQL check uses a read-only SELECT query to help you connect the lesson idea to the results table.
help.hint_1: Start with SELECT, choose the requested column or columns, then name the students table with FROM.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table with the prompt.

ci-show-name-and-city.title: Guided check: show two student details
ci-show-name-and-city.prompt: Complete a simple SELECT query that returns the `name` and `city` columns from the `students` table. Use the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
ci-show-name-and-city.hint: Follow the SELECT ... FROM ... pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This guided SQL check uses a read-only SELECT query to help you connect the lesson idea to the results table.
help.hint_1: Start with SELECT, choose the requested column or columns, then name the students table with FROM.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table with the prompt.

single-table-start.title: A single table can answer simple questions
single-table-start.bodyMarkdown:
A **table** stores rows of similar things. In this lesson's dataset, the `students` table stores one row per student.

```sql
SELECT *
FROM students;
```

This query means:
- `SELECT *` = show all columns
- `FROM students` = get the data from the `students` table

If you run it in the SQL editor and click **Run query**, the results table shows rows like Noor, Mia, and Leo.

A single table works well when all the information you need belongs to the same kind of thing. Here, each student already has a name, grade, age, and city in one place.

**Worked example:** If you want to see all student names and grades, one table is enough because both columns are already in `students`.

```sql
SELECT name, grade
FROM students;
```

Line by line:
- `SELECT name, grade` asks for only those two columns
- `FROM students` tells SQL which table to read

**Guided check:** Run the query, then change `grade` to `city` and check how the results table changes.

why-multiple-tables.title: Why databases sometimes use multiple tables
why-multiple-tables.bodyMarkdown:
A database often uses **multiple tables** when one big table would repeat the same information again and again.

Imagine a school database that stores students and classes. If every student row also repeated the full class details, the same class information could appear many times.

That is why databases often split information into separate tables:
- one table for students
- another table for classes
- sometimes another table for enrollments or relationships

In this module, our dataset has only one real table: `students`. So we can still talk about the idea of multiple tables without writing join queries yet.

**Worked example:** In the current `students` table, the city `Chicago` appears for more than one student. Repeated values are normal, but if a table had many columns about each city, repeating all of them in every student row would be wasteful.

```sql
SELECT city
FROM students;
```

This query shows that some values repeat across rows.

Line by line:
- `SELECT city` asks for one column
- `FROM students` reads from the student table

Multiple tables help organize data when different kinds of things need their own home.

**Guided check:** Look at the schema browser for `students`. Which columns describe the student directly, and which values might be repeated across many rows?

choosing-structure.title: How to think about one table vs many
choosing-structure.bodyMarkdown:
A good beginner rule is:
- use **one table** when all columns describe the same kind of thing
- use **multiple tables** when the data mixes different kinds of things or repeats large chunks of information

With our dataset, `id`, `name`, `grade`, `age`, and `city` all describe a student, so one table makes sense for this small example.

**Worked example:** This query asks for student names only.

```sql
SELECT name
FROM students;
```

That works because the table is focused on one kind of thing: students.

If you later wanted to store teachers, classrooms, and courses, putting everything into one giant table would become confusing. Separate tables would usually be clearer.

When you are deciding on structure, ask:
1. What is one row supposed to represent?
2. Do all columns describe that same thing?
3. Am I repeating lots of details that might belong somewhere else?

**Guided check:** In the SQL editor, run `SELECT id, name, city FROM students;` and ask yourself: does each row still clearly represent one student? If yes, that is a sign the table design is still focused.
